title: 29 Nehase — The Triple Feast & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "29 Nehase — The Triple Feast & The Assembly

narrative": "Nehase

The Triple Feast: Maskal, Qal and Timqat (Monthly Commemoration)

St. John the Baptist (Beheading of)

The Forty Martyrs of Sebaste

The twenty-ninth day of Nehase carries the monthly Triple Feast, the simultaneous commemoration of Maskal (the Finding of the True Cross), Qal (the Covenant/Word), and Timqat (the Theophany/Baptism of the Lord) — three of the most exalted feasts of the Ethiopian liturgical year gathered into a single monthly observance that is unique to the Tewahedo tradition. This triple commemoration speaks to the Ethiopian Church's understanding that the central mysteries of salvation are not isolated events but a single luminous reality seen from three angles: the Cross by which we are redeemed, the Word by which we are addressed and summoned, and the Baptism by which we are incorporated into the death and resurrection of the Lord. Monthly these three are held up together as a triptych, a threefold icon of the one economy.

The Beheading of St. John the Baptist falls on this twenty-ninth day with a gravity that the Ethiopian calendar feels fully. John was the last and greatest of the prophets, the one who stood on the boundary between the Old Covenant and the New, the voice crying in the wilderness who had the singular honor of baptizing the Lord he proclaimed. His execution at the pleasure of a dancing girl's birthday wish is one of the most disturbing events in the Gospel narratives — a reminder that the Kingdom of God does not come in power and triumph but in apparent failure and ignominy, that the greatest of those born of women was beheaded in a dungeon for the sake of an adulterer's pride. The Church mourns John's beheading as a fast-day in many traditions, and the Ethiopian calendar holds it in deep solemnity.

The Forty Martyrs of Sebaste add a corporate dimension of incomparable power to this penultimate day of Nehase. Forty soldiers of the Roman legion stationed at Sebaste in Armenia, commanded under pain of death to sacrifice to the gods, instead stripped off their armor and walked out onto the frozen lake, refusing. Heaters were placed on the bank to tempt them to recant; one did, and ran to the bank, but the guard watching was so struck by what he saw — reportedly, a vision of crowns descending from heaven toward the remaining thirty-nine — that he stripped off his own clothes, declared himself a Christian, and took the fortieth place on the ice. They died of exposure, singing. The image of forty men singing on a frozen lake in the face of death is one of the Church's most perfect icons of the freedom that the Gospel gives.

On this twenty-ninth and penultimate day of Nehase, the Triple Feast, the Beheading of the Forerunner, and the Martyrs of Sebaste converge in a great act of liturgical recapitulation. The month has been a journey through the Cross (the monthly Crucifixion on the twenty-seventh), the Word (present throughout in the prophets and apostles), and the Baptism (present in every martyrdom, which is a baptism of blood). Tomorrow Nehase ends. But today, the Church stands at the summit of the month's final ascent and sees the whole of it spread out below: the light of Tabor, the long fast, the Assumption's glory, the great saints, the martyrs, the patriarchs — all of it gathered up and offered back to God in the one unending act of worship that the liturgical year enacts.
